did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a shallow artificial neural network (ANN) with fully connected layers for the classification of skin cancer (benign vs. malignant) in Reflectance Confocal Microscopy (RCM) images. It also employs Speeded up Robust Features (SURF) for feature extraction and K-Means clustering for visual word formation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a methodology for classifying skin cancer confocal images using a shallow artificial neural network and visual words. It includes specific sections on Methodology (augmentation, feature extraction, modeling, classification), Experimental Results (dataset description, accuracy metrics in Table 2), and Conclusion. It was published in the proceedings of the 11th Hellenic Conference on Artificial Intelligence (SETN 2020).

title: Explainable Fully Connected Visual Words for the Classification of Skin Cancer Confocal Images: Interpreting the influence of visual words in classifying benign vs malignant pattern
brief_summary: This study proposes an explainable method for classifying benign versus malignant skin cancer patterns in Reflectance Confocal Microscopy (RCM) images using a shallow artificial neural network with fully connected layers and a visual vocabulary derived from Speeded up Robust Features (SURF). The dataset, sourced from the Andreas Syggros Hospital, initially contained 136 RCM images which were augmented to 408 samples using contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization and non-local means denoising. Classification performance was evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation, with K-Means clustering employed to form the visual words. The results demonstrate that automated classification of RCM images can effectively assist dermatologists by reducing the burden of manual interpretation and providing interpretable insights into the visual patterns responsible for diagnostic outcomes.

lesion_types_studied.details: Group of benign samples in the dataset, totaling 90 images. Subtypes include Seborrheic Keratosis, Solar Lentigo, and Nevus.
Group of malignant samples in the dataset, totaling 46 images. Subtypes include Spitz, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, and Lentigo Maligna-Lentigo Maligna Melanoma.
Specific benign lesion type included in the dataset of 90 benign samples.

Specific malignant lesion type included in the dataset of 46 malignant samples. Mentioned in introduction as the most common form of Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer.
Specific malignant lesion type included in the dataset of 46 malignant samples. Figure 3a depicts an actinic keratosis skin cancer.
Specific malignant lesion type included in the dataset of 46 malignant samples.

Mentioned in the introduction as the most lethal form of skin cancer and in the dataset description as part of the malignant group (LM-MM). The study focuses on a binary classification of benign vs. malignant rather than specific melanoma subtypes.
Mentioned in the introduction as a main category of skin cancer, divided into BCCs and SCCs. The study's malignant class includes non-melanoma types like BCC and AK.
Mentioned in the introduction as the second most common form of skin cancer. Not explicitly listed as a specific class in the experimental dataset description.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (benign vs. malignant skin cancer patterns in RCM images)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'binary classification' to distinguish between benign and malignant skin cancer patterns using Reflectance Confocal Microscopy (RCM) images. The model predicts discrete classes (benign or malignant) using a shallow artificial neural network with a Softmax output layer and Cosine proximity loss function. The dataset consists of 90 benign and 46 malignant samples.

datasets_used.name: Andreas Syggros Hospital RCM Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of Reflectance Confocal Microscopy (RCM) images provided by the Andreas Syggros Hospital of Cutaneous and Venereal Diseases in Athens. Images were captured using a Mavig Vivascope 3000 operating at 830nm, providing images up to a depth of 200µm.

use.training: The initial dataset of 136 images was augmented (tripled in size) to 408 images using Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization and Non-Local Means Denoising. This augmented dataset was used for training the shallow artificial neural network via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used within the 10-fold cross-validation scheme to avoid overfitting.
use.testing: Used within the 10-fold cross-validation scheme for evaluation.
use.other: The dataset was also used for the interpretation/explanation task to visualize influential visual patterns (SURF interest points) responsible for classification outcomes.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Applied to avoid overfitting of the predictive model and to evaluate classification performance.
Used to measure clustering performance and determine the optimal number of clusters (k=345) for the visual vocabulary.
Used to determine the number of epochs for training the neural network.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported classification accuracy metrics using 10-fold cross-validation and used the Silhouette index for clustering evaluation.

Andreas Syggros Hospital RCM Dataset.notes: The highest accuracy achieved was 91.17% using K-Means Canopy clustering with SURF and Haralick features and Cosine Proximity loss function. The paper notes that adding Haralick global texture information slightly improves performance. The Silhouette index was used to determine the optimal number of clusters (k=345). Results are based on 10-fold cross-validation.

architecture_used.main_model: Shallow artificial neural network (ANN) / Vanilla fully connected neural network
architecture_used.input_size: k+14 neurons (where k is the number of visual words/clusters, determined as k=345 via Silhouette index; total input dimension 359)

architecture_details.layers: Single hidden layer structure implied by 'simple vanilla neural network' and 'absence of many layers'; Input layer (k+14) connected to Output layer (2)

classification_or_output_head.loss_function: Cosine proximity (best performing); Negative Log Likelihood also tested

preprocessing_techniques: Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for image enhancement
Non-Local (NL) Means Denoising for artifact removal
Data augmentation by tripling the dataset size using the above transformations

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used for image enhancement to mitigate sample sparsity. Applied to the initial dataset of 136 RCM images.
Used for artifact removal. Applied to the initial dataset of 136 RCM images.
The initial dataset of 136 images was augmented to 408 images by applying the above two transformations, effectively tripling the sample size.

conclusions: The proposed methodology, which combines Speeded up Robust Features (SURF) with a shallow artificial neural network, achieves a highest test set accuracy of 91.17% using K-Means Canopy clustering and Cosine Proximity loss function.
The classification results are claimed to be the highest reported in literature for RCM images at the time of publication.
Adding Haralick global texture information to the feature vector slightly improves the performance of the classifier.

The interpretation scheme, based on Visual Pattern Weighted Localization and Visual Word Weight Calculation, provides plausible explanations for classification outcomes by identifying influential visual patterns (interest points).
The explainability module successfully identified key visual patterns, such as irregular keratinocytes, which correspond to the highest influence on the classifier's decision for malignant cases.
The visualization results of the interpretation scheme were validated qualitatively by collaborating dermatologists.
The study demonstrates that automated classification of RCM images can relieve the time-consuming burden from dermatologists and save patients from invasive biopsy procedures.
The current dataset size (408 augmented samples) is insufficient to definitively rule out overfitting; more samples are needed to shape a solid opinion about the classifier's performance.
Future work will involve expanding the dataset to enable the application of data-demanding deep learning techniques.
The absence of many layers in the ANN allows the model to be trained on standard desktop hardware (Intel Core i5 CPU, 8GB RAM).
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small dataset (136 original images, augmented to 408), which the authors explicitly acknowledge is insufficient to definitively rule out overfitting.
The dataset is imbalanced, containing 90 benign samples versus 46 malignant samples.

The malignant class is heterogeneous, combining Melanoma (Lentigo Maligna) and Non-Melanoma Skin Cancers (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Spitz), which may obscure subtype-specific diagnostic performance.

The model is prone to misinterpreting 'skin fold' patterns as malignancy, leading to erroneous predictions for benign lesions with honeycomb patterns, highlighting a specific failure mode of the visual word approach.
The paper claims the results are the highest reported in literature for RCM images at the time, but lacks external validation on independent datasets.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; performance was evaluated solely via 10-fold cross-validation accuracy.

The interpretability module is integrated in-model, allowing for direct visualization of influential visual patterns (SURF keypoints), which was qualitatively validated by collaborating dermatologists.
The study uses a shallow artificial neural network rather than deep learning, which limits its capacity to handle large-scale data but allows training on standard desktop hardware.
